title: India's defence conundrum is Russia over-dependence
author: Manish Tewari
brief: The penultimate decade of the twentieth century and the first two decades of the 21st demonstrated to the United States that the only constant in the geo-strategic calculus is the ``unknown unknown'' and that is what it must prepare for. It is the unknown that the US is trying to hedge against as it seeks to build alliances like the Quad and AUKUS. And yet, India's defence indigenisation programme is still in its infancy.

title: Will signing Artemis Accords benefit India?
author: Aditya Ramanathan
brief: India must overcome domestic resistance to collaboration with other space agencies and develop legislation that encourages space activities. Signing the Artemis Accords alone has no financial implications for India but if the country wants to be a major player in the Artemis programme, ISRO will need a significant hike in its annual budget.

title: Why are Indian drugmakers under the lens?
author: Maitri Porecha
brief: Merely suspending or cancelling the manufacturing licence of a pharma company is not enough. Under the Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940, manufacturers not adhering to good manufacturing practices can be subjected to a maximum punishment of imprisonment for life. However the poor conviction rate was due to glaring errors committed by drug inspectors. If India wants to redeem its reputation, it will have to tighten the screws by ensuring robust pharma inspections and make sure that any slipup by manufacturers is reported and prosecuted.

title: What is China's new law on foreign relations?
author: Ananth Krishnan
brief: New Law on Foreign Relations of the People�s Republic of China, which came into effect on July 1, may narrow the scope for resolution of territorial disputes. The law will tighten President Xi Jinping�s control over foreign policy. This centralised foreign policy lays emphasis on ``sovereignty and security'' as being at the heart of Chinese foreign policy. It is seen as a legal response to Western sanctions aimed at China.

title: Jamia Masjid: Witness to a turbulent history
author: Bashaarat Masood
brief: Since 2008, the Jamia Masjid or the `Grand Mosque', the centre of religion and politics in Kashmir, has often been shutdown for worshippers by successive governments to ``prevent anti-government protests'' in the Valley. It has been a bellwether for the political situation in the Valley.

title: When they doubted our frydom
author: Indrajit Hazra
brief: There is an entrenched racism against `non-Western' dishes that some Westerners could not bear the fact that India, and other countries of `Global South India' (like Sri Lanka), have a rich tradition of preparing, serving and consuming fish, with chips, too.

title: Why today's NRIs reflect old-fashioned India more than urban resident Indians
author: Anuvab Pal
brief: Being in the West, once such an aspiration for Indians, is no longer a ready sign of Westernisation. In fact, it could be the opposite, with sections in Indian cities far more Westernised than many desis living abroad.

title: Microfinance and financial health
author: Jiji Mammen
brief: Currently, the expansion of microfinance in India is skewed. India must take cue from Bangladesh's Palli Karma Sahayak Foundation if it is to take care of the funding needs of MFIs, especially the smaller ones. This can bolster microfinance's reach in the country.

title: A vote for cooperative federalism
author: Mukesh Butani
Shankey Agrawal
brief: The technology-driven compliant system of GST network is a clear uplift for the business confidence index. It has helped weed out inefficiencies in the system leading to the formalisation of economy. This is likely to have a trickle-down effect by way of increased collection of taxes. Anti-profiteering provisions in the GST law is an area of concern.

title: As Manipur burns
author: Pravesh Jain
brief: Manipur's history is marked by a struggle for self-determination and a desire for greater autonomy. These include the Meiteis, Nagas, Kukis, Chins, Manipuri Muslims, Aimol, Kom, Anal, and others. The presence of numerous insurgent groups in Manipur has also contributed significantly to the violence. Reports of extra-judicial killings, enforced disappearances, and arbitrary detentions by security forces have raised serious concerns.

title: Society of the future- a new paradigm
author: Basab Dasgupta
brief: Two major social transformations are taking place throughout the world that will initiate profound and permanent changes in how human society evolves in the future are- the LGBTQ+ movement and Artificial Intelligence. It is mathematically obvious that if the percentage of the heterosexual population as a fraction of the total population drops below a certain threshold, the world's population will start to decrease. With AI, a handful of mega-corporations will make so much profit from this headcount reduction that they will be able to pay all laid-off employees a monthly allowance.

title: Unnerving consequences
author: Shantanu Mukharji
brief: While shared geopolitical objectives mainly revolve around China, India is not inclined to be classified as a �US ally�. Multiple factors, primarily a nationalistic ethos and awareness of its weight and size, prevent India from acting as a US �yes man� in Asia.

title: A code fair to all faiths and genders is more important than UCC
author: Zakia Soman
Shriya Maini
brief: A just and equal code- for all genders, religions and faiths in India- is far more important than a uniform code. Awareness and education of the people regarding the objectives of UCC and its pace of implementation are two key discussion points. A piecemeal fashion of gradual implementation similar to the Goa model could be more acceptable. But a nation as complex as ours calls for an endeavour beyond politics to inclusion, collaboration and judicious deliberation.

title: A disquieting trend in governor's actions
author: P D T Achary
brief: The governor is a high constitutional functionary who is required to act with exemplary impartiality and objectivity. Tamil Nadu governor RN Ravi�s actions should serve as a reminder that governors have limited powers and very little discretion under the Indian Constitution.

title: Would-be winners in Ukraine war are lurching towards far right in despair
author: Saeed Naqvii
brief: Popular will is anathema to most entrenched establishments. The consent has to be fashioned. This continuous tussle between establishments and the people leads to one result: Muscular establishments emerge on top. The popular will is the missing ingredient in what are advertised as democracies. We are living in hollowed-out democracies. The politicians, corporates and TV channels are together in diverting the poor away from their condition towards the glorious inheritance they've been wilfully denied by ``The Other''.

title: Check governors trying to exceed their powers
author: Nilanjan Mukhopadhyay
brief: The BJP appointed two different types of governors. In the first category were those in BJP-ruled states, while the others were the individuals in Opposition-ruled states. But more than the appointment, what is particularly condemnable is the way that governors have locked horns with duly elected governments in the states governed by Opposition parties and how they have been encouraged by the Centre. All this squarely adds up to the conclusion that this government pays scant respect to the federal principle despite the PM�s initial clarion call on the idea of cooperative federalism.
